articles.title: Body of Seyyed Ali Khamenei brought to farewell ceremony in Tehran
articles.summary: Body of Seyyed Ali Khamenei brought to farewell ceremony in Tehran The body of Iran's supreme leader Ayatollah Seyyed Ali Khamenei arrives at the Imam Khomeini Hussainiya, southern Tehran, for the farewell rite, IRNA reported. More than 20 million people are reportedly expecte...
articles.content:
The body of Iran's supreme leader Ayatollah Seyyed Ali Khamenei arrives at the Imam Khomeini Hussainiya, southern Tehran, for the farewell rite, IRNA reported.

More than 20 million people are reportedly expected to travel to Iran’s capital for the funeral of Ali Khamenei, who was assassinated by the US and Israel on 28 February, according to Iranian state TV.

This would more than double Tehran’s population of roughly 10 million.

The video shows the moment the body of martyred Leader of the Islamic Revolution Ayatollah Seyyed Ali Khamenei arrives at the Imam Khomeini Hussainiya, southern Tehran, for the farewell rite. pic.twitter.com/KiZLj4IEjU

— IRNA News Agency ☫ (@IrnaEnglish) July 2, 2026

articles.title: Iran war live: Tehran slams US before huge funeral for Ali Khamenei
articles.summary: Lebanon's Health Ministry says at least 4,298 people have been killed and 12,196 injured in Israeli attacks since March.
articles.content:
Iran’s Foreign Ministry spokesman, Esmaeil Baghaei, said the US has “constantly demonstrated its complete contempt for genuine peace and security in West Asia”, warning regional countries to draw “clear lessons” from the US-Israel war on Iran.

More than 20 million people are expected in Tehran for the funeral of former Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, who was killed at the start of the US-Israel war on Iran.

articles.title: Trump: US removed 22 tankers from Hormuz ‘in complete radio silence’
articles.summary: Trump: US removed 22 tankers from Hormuz ‘in complete radio silence’ The US president said the tankers were moved under heavy military protection and “complete radio silence” in a single night as part of a month-and-a-half military operation, claiming the operation helped prev...
articles.content:
The US president said the tankers were moved under heavy military protection and “complete radio silence” in a single night as part of a month-and-a-half military operation, claiming the operation helped prevent global oil prices from surging.

"If we hadn't done it, a barrel of oil would have gone to $350," Trump told CNBC, adding that he did not want his presidency associated with triggering a global economic downturn, as the late US president Herbert Hoover did.

Trump also claimed the US military had achieved a "complete military victory" over Iran in the past four months, saying Tehran had been "completely defeated militarily" and that any remaining Iranian missile capabilities "can be destroyed".

He said US forces carried out powerful strikes against Iran for three consecutive nights over the past two weeks in retaliation for a drone attack on a ship, without providing further details.

Trump claimed that American forces destroyed a new Iranian radar system last week, leaving Iran "without any radar network". He dismissed reports suggesting Tehran had restored or strengthened its military capabilities, insisting that "Iran's military power is finished".

articles.title: China ramps up warning to Japan, Philippines over maritime talks
articles.summary: Japan and the Philippines have suggested they would likely be drawn into any fight over Taiwan.
articles.content:
China ramps up warning to Japan, Philippines over maritime talks

  • China legally warned Japan and the Philippines against their maritime border talks, calling them "internationally wrongful acts" as they proceeded without consulting Beijing.
  • China claims exclusive economic rights over the area, including Taiwan, and has increased Coast Guard patrols and seabed surveys near the disputed waters.
  • Japan and the Philippines have strengthened military ties and in recent months suggested they would likely be drawn into any fight over Taiwan.

AI generated

BEIJING – China issued a legal warning against Japan and the Philippines over their upcoming maritime border talks, escalating pressure on Washington’s key regional allies as they attempt to counter Beijing’s naval assertiveness.

The Chinese Ministry of Natural Resources said the planned discussions “constitute an internationally wrongful act” as it urged the two nations to start talks with Beijing, according to a legal opinion it released on July 2.

The talks are unlawful as they are initiated “without consultation with China”, read the document issued by the China Institute for Marine Affairs under the ministry. The report also warned outside powers to avoid the “political risks” of assisting or recognising the talks.

The move signals Beijing’s growing displeasure at Manila and Tokyo’s decision to start maritime border talks involving waters east of Taiwan. China has subsequently sent Coast Guard vessels to patrol the area, as well as survey ships to map the seabed.

The legal opinion said China owns exclusive economic zone and continental shelf in the region, as its territory covers Taiwan. The government in Taipei rejects that view, insisting that Taiwan is de facto independent.

The Japan-Philippine talks “in essence ignored the fact that Taiwan and its subordinate islands belong to China”, which infringe upon China’s territorial sovereignty and violate the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea, the legal opinion read.

The leaders of Japan and the Philippines have suggested in recent months their nations would likely be drawn into any fight over Taiwan, remarks that irked Beijing.

The two have also moved to deepen military ties, agreeing in May to elevate their relationship and open a series of defence talks. BLOOMBERG

articles.title: Iran's Gharibabadi: 3 July reminder of crime in which American took 290 lives
articles.summary: Iran's Gharibabadi: 3 July reminder of crime in which American took 290 lives Iran’s deputy foreign minister Kazem Gharibabadi wrote on X that "July 3rd is a reminder of the crime in which America took the lives of 290 innocent people, including 66 children”. “This crime will...
articles.content:
Iran's Gharibabadi: 3 July reminder of crime in which American took 290 lives

Iran’s deputy foreign minister Kazem Gharibabadi wrote on X that "July 3rd is a reminder of the crime in which America took the lives of 290 innocent people, including 66 children”.

“This crime will never be erased from the memory of the Iranian nation. The second crime began after the shooting down of the plane: denying responsibility, failing to issue an official apology, and awarding a medal to the commander of the criminal ship,” Gharibabadi said.

Iran Air Flight 655 was en route from Bandar Abbas to Dubai on 3 July 1988 when it was shot down over the Persian Gulf by the USS Vincennes.

The United States said the aircraft had been mistaken for an attacking military jet, but never issued a formal apology, maintaining that the crew acted in self-defence.

In 1996, Washington agreed to pay compensation to the victims' families as part of a settlement at the International Court of Justice without admitting legal liability.

In another tweet, Gharibabadi said that “alongside commemorating the martyrs of Flight 655, the Iranian nation honours the memory and name of its martyred mujahid Imam.”

“The martyrdom of Imam Khamenei, the immensely dignified Leader of the Revolution, serves for Iranians as a symbol of the continuity and depth of America's enmity toward the Iranian nation, and also as an enduring symbol of their steadfastness and resistance against American enmity.”

این روزها، در کنار یاد شهدای پرواز ۶۵۵، ملت ایران یاد و نام امام مجاهد شهید خود را گرامی می‌دارد. شهادت امام خامنه‌ای، قائد عظیم الشان انقلاب، برای ایرانیان نماد استمرار و عمق دشمنی آمریکا با ملت ایران و همچنین، نماد همیشگی ایستادگی و مقاومت آنان در برابر دشمنی آمریکاست. (۲)

— Gharibabadi (@Gharibabadi) July 2, 2026

articles.title: Brazil implements new regulations for meat exports to EU, Globo Rural reports
articles.summary: SAO PAULO, July 2 - Brazil's Agriculture Ministry has started to implement new inspection procedures on the production of meat and meat derivatives to address trade requirements from the European Union, local news outlet Globo Rural reported on Thursday, citing a government do...
articles.content:
Brazil implements new regulations for meat exports to EU, Globo Rural reports

SAO PAULO, July 2 - Brazil's Agriculture Ministry has started to implement new inspection procedures on the production of meat and meat derivatives to address trade requirements from the European Union, local news outlet Globo Rural reported on Thursday, citing a government document sent to federal agriculture auditors.

• Brazil seeks to prevent the EU from banning imports of some Brazilian products, including beef, poultry, eggs and live animals, starting in September.

• The ban, announced in May, is linked to EU rules restricting the use of certain antimicrobial substances in food-producing animals.

• Brazil's Agriculture Ministry did not immediately respond to Reuters' request for comment. REUTERS

articles.title: US officials believed Israel was plotting to kill Iranian negotiators
articles.summary: Killing senior Iranian leaders had been part of Israel’s strategy from the start of the war.
articles.content:
US officials believed Israel was plotting to kill Iranian negotiators

Julian E. Barnes, Farnaz Fassihi

  • US officials believed Israel planned to assassinate Iranian negotiators Abbas Araghchi and Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf during peace talks to derail negotiations.
  • Israel targeted Iran's leadership early in the war, aiming for regime change, while US sought a peace deal and warned Israel to avoid striking negotiators.
  • Iran took security measures against Israeli attacks, including escorted travel and emergency landings, as negotiations continued towards a ceasefire and future talks.

AI generated

WASHINGTON – United States officials believed that Israel might have been plotting to kill Iran’s top negotiators while Washington was engaged with Tehran in delicate talks this spring to reach an interim peace deal, according to current and former US officials.

Killing senior Iranian leaders had been part of Israel’s strategy from the start of the war. But America’s concerns about the targeting of two particular Iranian officials – Abbas Araghchi, Iran’s foreign minister, and Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf, speaker of parliament – spiked during delicate ceasefire negotiations that began in April.

Fearful that an Israeli assassination effort would doom the negotiations, the US, according to some of the officials, went so far as to ask other countries in the region to warn Iran about the possibility Israel could target the two officials.

US officials acknowledged that during the intense phase of the war, Araghchi and Ghalibaf, as senior government officials, could have been legitimate targets for Israel, which was intent on toppling Iran’s hard-line government.

But after the negotiations started in earnest in April, US officials believed any attempt to kill the Iranian leaders would end the talks and reignite the fighting.

The war began on Feb 28 with an Israeli strike that killed Iran’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and other top officials, based in part on US intelligence.

While US strikes focused on Iran’s navy and missile forces, Israel prioritised targeting the leadership in the early phase of the war, intent on killing as many high-ranking officials as it could.

That included killing potentially more pragmatic leaders that the Trump administration had hoped to negotiate with, such as Ali Larijani, Iran’s top national security official, and Kamal Kharazi, a former Iranian foreign minister. Both men were involved in the negotiations with the US when they were killed in Israeli airstrikes.

The Trump administration’s suspicions about the possible Israeli plot to kill the two top negotiators show how the US and Israeli war aims, which were close at the very beginning of the war, quickly diverged radically.

And while the US wanted a peace agreement, Israel has been sceptical from the initial cessation of hostilities in April.

The initial two-week ceasefire in April was met with grudging Israeli official support and broad public concern in Israel that the US was ending the war too early. Rather than being driven from power, the theocratic government of Iran had become even more hard-line, and Iran’s Revolutionary Guard had only consolidated its control over the country.

Araghchi and Ghalibaf have been the key officials negotiating with various countries in the region to reach a ceasefire and then a more lasting peace with the US.

In June, the US and Iran reached a framework agreement that sought to open the Strait of Hormuz and set the outline for follow-on talks on Tehran’s nuclear programme.

Officials and commentators in Israel viewed the initial agreement as a disaster because it did not accomplish their country’s war aims of forcing regime change, destroying Iran’s proxy forces and seriously damaging its missile programme.

Israeli officials also worried the agreement would put billions of dollars into Iran, allowing it to quickly rebuild after the war and without meaningfully restricting its nuclear ambitions.

A spokesperson for the Israeli Embassy in Washington declined to comment.

Asked about Israeli plans and the warning to Iran, a US official noted that talks between American and Iranian delegations continue and that Steve Witkoff, a special envoy, and Jared Kushner, the president’s son-in-law, had productive meetings in Qatar. President Donald Trump, the official said, wants the peace process “to play out”.

The Wall Street Journal reported in March that Israel had Araghchi and Ghalibaf on a target list, but temporarily removed them as the US discussed beginning negotiations with Iran.

A US official and a Middle East official said the Trump administration had learnt around that time that at least Ghalibaf was on an Israeli targeting list and asked Israel to refrain.

Ghalibaf was nearly killed in both the 12-day war in June 2025 and again in the 2026 conflict, when Israel targeted a secret meeting of senior government officials in a bunker under a mountain, according to three senior Iranian officials and public comments by officials. In both incidents, Ghalibaf was rescued from under the rubble, the officials said.

During the negotiations, Iran has taken precautions aimed at making it more difficult for Israel to strike at senior officials.

In April, Ghalibaf was set to travel to Islamabad to meet with Vice-President J.D. Vance. But Iranian security officials were concerned that Israel would use the opportunity to assassinate Ghalibaf or Araghchi to derail the talks, the officials said.

Iranians sought guarantees from the US, through Pakistani and Qatari intermediaries, that Israel would not carry out any covert operations targeting the Iranian delegation, the officials said.

Pakistani fighter jets escorted the Iranian aircraft carrying a delegation of more than 70 Iranians from the border of Iran to Islamabad and back again when the session was over.

But on the way back to Tehran, an Israeli security threat emerged.

Iran’s security forces notified the plane carrying Ghalibaf back to Tehran that they had picked up intelligence that Israel planned to attack the plane and that two Israeli fighter jets had entered Iran’s airspace from its western border near Iraq, the two officials said.

Mahdi Mohammadi, a senior adviser for Ghalibaf, who accompanied him to Islamabad, confirmed this account on his social media page. The plane made an emergency landing in the city of Mashhad, Iran’s closest airport to the Pakistani border, and the Iranian delegation travelled some eight hours by land back to Tehran, Mohammadi and the two officials said.

But the officials have continued to travel.

In late May, Ghalibaf and Araghchi flew to Qatar for talks and then travelled to Switzerland in June for a second in-person meeting with Vance and the American delegation. NYTIMES

  • Aaron Boxerman contributed reporting from Jerusalem.

articles.title: Iran envoy: US military bases in the Gulf caused ‘nothing but insecurity’
articles.summary: Iran envoy: US military bases in the Gulf caused ‘nothing but insecurity’ Iranian Ambassador to the UN Amir-Saeid Iravani said on Thursday that US military bases in the Gulf states have brought “nothing but instability” to the region, in remarks delivered during an emergency s...
articles.content:
Iranian Ambassador to the UN Amir-Saeid Iravani said on Thursday that US military bases in the Gulf states have brought “nothing but instability” to the region, in remarks delivered during an emergency session of the Security Council.

“The US has no coastal border in our region. It is not their waters. It is not their neighbourhood,” Iravani said.

He pointed out the hypocrisy of Security Council members blaming Tehran for violence and instability in the region whilst ignoring the role of the US and Israel.

“Instead of addressing the root cause of the current crisis, they have ignored the unlawful aggression committed against Iran, remained silent in the face of these serious violations, and sought to shift blame onto the victim,” Iravani added.

articles.title: Gaza's breast cancer patients face immediate mastectomies as doctors plead for end to blockade
articles.summary: Gaza's breast cancer patients face immediate mastectomies as doctors plead for end to blockade Physicians and health professionals in Gaza marked 1,000 days of genocide on Thursday with a plea for international pressure to lift Israel's nearly two-decade blockade, as well as i...
articles.content:
Gaza's breast cancer patients face immediate mastectomies as doctors plead for end to blockade

Physicians and health professionals in Gaza marked 1,000 days of genocide on Thursday with a plea for international pressure to lift Israel's nearly two-decade blockade, as well as its ban on critical medical supplies for Palestinians that even include chlorine bleach to disinfect hospitals.

In a virtual call hosted by the global non-profit Doctors Against Genocide, a slate of experts described conditions that can barely sustain the healthiest residents of Gaza, let alone those who are elderly, disabled, or with illnesses that predate the 7 October 2023 Hamas-led attacks on southern Israel, and the subsequent genocide in Gaza.

Israel's assault has so far killed 1,700 medical workers, wholly or partly destroyed 38 hospitals, and hit nearly 200 ambulances in the enclave, the Gaza health ministry has said.

Women with breast cancer are undergoing immediate mastectomies because it's the fastest way to save their lives when there are no deeper diagnostic tools or cancer drugs, Dr Ahmed Shatat, director general of International Cooperation and Planning in Gaza, said on the call.

"Previously, we were holding seven MRI machines. Now we have not even one MRI machine - zero - in the Gaza Strip," he added.

articles.title: Hind Rajab Foundation calls on top prosecutor to investigate Ben Gvir ahead of NYC visit
articles.summary: Hind Rajab Foundation calls on top prosecutor to investigate Ben Gvir ahead of NYC visit The Hind Rajab Foundation (HRF), in partnership with the Center for Constitutional Rights (CCR), sent a letter to New York state attorney general Letitia James on Thursday, urging her to o...
articles.content:
Hind Rajab Foundation calls on top prosecutor to investigate Ben Gvir ahead of NYC visit

The Hind Rajab Foundation (HRF), in partnership with the Center for Constitutional Rights (CCR), sent a letter to New York state attorney general Letitia James on Thursday, urging her to open a criminal investigation into Israeli Minister of National Security Itamar Ben Gvir.

Ben Gvir, notorious for his openly violent rhetoric against Palestinians and Muslims, is expected to be in Manhattan next week for the Chiefs of Police Summit from 7-8 July.

The groups are also urging James to meet with what they say are local victims of Ben Gvir, including one Palestinian American subjected to starvation and torture in Israeli prison, as well as 10 participants in the 2025 and 2026 Global Sumud Flotillas who were subjected to abuse directly by Ben Gvir.

"The ball is in her court; she needs to meet with these people," Jake Romm, the US representative for HRF, told Middle East Eye on Thursday. "They are New Yorkers. They deserve the protection of the law, and she has an obligation to bring justice."

HRF called on James to open an "Atrocity Crimes Accountability Task Force" in the criminal justice division, specifically for victims of Israeli officials and the state's policies.

James's office operates independently of political leadership, but she is a strong ally of New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who has been an outspoken critic of Israel's policies.

articles.title: Russian attacks kill three in eastern Ukraine, officials say
articles.summary: A seven-year-old child was killed in an attack and two other children were injured.
articles.content:
Russian attacks kill three in eastern Ukraine, officials say

  • Russian attacks on July 2 killed three people in eastern Ukraine, with casualties in Nikopol, Synelnykove and Oleksandrivka, according to regional officials.
  • A seven-year-old child was among the dead, and several others were injured in multiple bombings and attacks across the Donetsk and Dnipropetrovsk regions.
  • In Luhansk, a Ukrainian drone strike on a minibus injured nine people.

AI generated

Russian attacks killed three people on July 2 in different regions of eastern Ukraine, regional officials said.

One person died in a series of attacks around the town of Nikopol, a frequent Russian target lying on the opposite bank of the Dnipro River from the Russian-held Zaporizhzhia nuclear power plant, according to Dnipropetrovsk region Governor Oleksandr Hanzha.

Three people were injured.

A seven-year-old child was killed in an attack and two other children were injured further north-east near the town of Synelnykove, Hanzha said.

In the Donetsk region, focal point of many of the clashes along the 1,250km front line, Russian forces dropped seven bombs on the town of Oleksandrivka, killing one person and injuring two, Governor Vadym Filashkin said.

One person was killed south-west of the city of Donetsk, the Moscow-installed head of parts of the Donetsk Region under Russian control said.

And in the Luhansk region, which Russia says it fully controls, Moscow-appointed officials said a Ukrainian drone strike on a minibus injured nine people.

Reuters could not independently verify accounts from either side. REUTERS

articles.title: Hind Rajab Foundation calls on top prosecutor to investigate Ben Gvir ahead of NYC visit
articles.summary: Hind Rajab Foundation calls on top prosecutor to investigate Ben Gvir ahead of NYC visit The Israeli national security minister is expected to attend a United Nations conference next week Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben Gvir speaks to reporters in Jerusalem, on 1...
articles.content:
Hind Rajab Foundation calls on top prosecutor to investigate Ben Gvir ahead of NYC visit

The Hind Rajab Foundation (HRF), in partnership with the Center for Constitutional Rights (CCR), sent a letter to New York state attorney general Letitia James on Thursday, urging her to open a criminal investigation into Israeli Minister of National Security Itamar Ben Gvir.

Ben Gvir, notorious for his openly violent rhetoric against Palestinians and Muslims, is expected to be in Manhattan next week for the Chiefs of Police Summit from 7-8 July.

The groups are also urging James to meet with what they say are local victims of Ben Gvir, including one Palestinian American subjected to starvation and torture in Israeli prison, as well as 10 participants in the 2025 and 2026 Global Sumud Flotillas who were subjected to abuse directly by Ben Gvir.

"The ball is in her court; she needs to meet with these people," Jake Romm, the US representative for HRF, told Middle East Eye on Thursday. "They are New Yorkers. They deserve the protection of the law, and she has an obligation to bring justice."

HRF called on James to open an "Atrocity Crimes Accountability Task Force" in the criminal justice division, specifically for victims of Israeli officials and the state's policies.

James's office operates independently of political leadership, but she is a strong ally of New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who has been an outspoken critic of Israel's policies.

That dynamic could mean a more hospitable environment for HRF's request.

"In New York, I do think there's more of a political will," Romm said.

"We saw with the primaries these candidates' stances on Palestine played a major role in this election. It also played a major role in the mayoral election. And I mean truthfully, the majority of the country is against Israel's actions in Palestine and Lebanon," he added.

'Direct incitement to genocide'

Ben Gvir himself was sanctioned by the Biden administration for his encouragement of deadly settler violence in the occupied West Bank. Among the Trump administration's first moves last year was the undoing of the punitive measures against him.

In its urging of an investigation, HRF's letter further cites Ben Gvir’s distribution of gun permits and weapons to Israeli settlers in the occupied West Bank "in furtherance of Israel’s policies of apartheid and violent dispossession", as well as his "direct incitement to genocide" in Gaza, where more than 73,000 Palestinians have been killed by Israel since the Hamas-led attacks on 7 October 2023.

"There are reasonable grounds to believe that he has committed at least nine different crimes under the New York Penal Code," HRF said in its press release.

"Israeli officials like Ben Gvir enjoy near complete impunity. When [US President Donald] Trump is complicit and works to foster that impunity, it is imperative that New York State officials act to challenge it," Brad Parker, CCR's associate director of policy, noted in that press release.

CCR is a progressive legal advocacy non-profit based in New York City, and has taken on the cases of several of the pro-Palestine students who have been targeted by the Trump administration.

HRF is a two-year-old organisation that has brought cases against state officials, senior commanders, as well as lower-ranking soldiers accused of being complicit in Israel's genocide in Gaza.

articles.title: Iranian foreign minister, UN chief hold call to discuss regional developments
articles.summary: Iranian foreign minister, UN chief hold call to discuss regional developments Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and United Nations secretary general Antonio Guterres held a phone call on Thursday amid ongoing US-Iran negotiations in Qatar. The officials discussed the lat...
articles.content:
Iranian foreign minister, UN chief hold call to discuss regional developments

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi and United Nations secretary general Antonio Guterres held a phone call on Thursday amid ongoing US-Iran negotiations in Qatar.

The officials discussed the latest regional developments, the Strait of Hormuz, and the agreement to end the “military aggression” of the US and Israel, according to Iran’s ministry.

articles.title: Jerusalem governorate warns against Israeli approval of 13 new settlements
articles.summary: Jerusalem governorate warns against Israeli approval of 13 new settlements The Jerusalem governorate released a statement on Thursday, warning against the implications of the Israeli security cabinet's approval of a plan to establish 13 new settlements in the occupied West Ban...
articles.content:
Jerusalem governorate warns against Israeli approval of 13 new settlements

The Jerusalem governorate released a statement on Thursday, warning against the implications of the Israeli security cabinet's approval of a plan to establish 13 new settlements in the occupied West Bank, which would further isolate Jerusalem.

The governorate said that the move reflects a wider Israeli policy of accelerating settlement expansion and land seizure, particularly during election periods, when, it said, Palestinian land and lives become part of Israeli political competition.

articles.title: Gaza's breast cancer patients face immediate mastectomies as doctors plead for end to blockade
articles.summary: Gaza's breast cancer patients face immediate mastectomies as doctors plead for end to blockade Revelations about the devastated health sector come as Gaza marks '1,000 days of genocide' since 7 October 2023 A man looks over the shrouded body of Palestinian child Malik Abu Shaw...
articles.content:
Gaza's breast cancer patients face immediate mastectomies as doctors plead for end to blockade

Physicians and health professionals in Gaza marked 1,000 days of genocide on Thursday with a plea for international pressure to lift Israel's nearly two-decade blockade, as well as its ban on critical medical supplies for Palestinians that even include chlorine bleach to disinfect hospitals.

In a virtual call hosted by the global non-profit Doctors Against Genocide, a slate of experts described conditions that can barely sustain the healthiest residents of Gaza, let alone those who are elderly, disabled, or with illnesses that predate the 7 October 2023 Hamas-led attacks on southern Israel, and the subsequent genocide in Gaza.

Israel's assault has so far killed 1,700 medical workers, wholly or partly destroyed 38 hospitals, and hit nearly 200 ambulances in the enclave, the Gaza health ministry has said.

Women with breast cancer are undergoing immediate mastectomies because it's the fastest way to save their lives when there are no deeper diagnostic tools or cancer drugs, Dr Ahmed Shatat, director general of International Cooperation and Planning in Gaza, said on the call.

"Previously, we were holding seven MRI machines. Now we have not even one MRI machine - zero - in the Gaza Strip," he added.

"Out of the cardiac catheterisation machines, we were handling seven. Now we are just working in one old machine... with no availability of a stent for catheterisation."

A catheter inserted into a blood vessel and guided to the heart can help diagnose a myriad of fairly common diseases, such as blockages that could lead to heart attacks and death.

But prevention, the best form of medicine, is no longer an option in a place that had prided itself on its medical infrastructure and its medical graduates, even through nearly two decades of Israeli siege.

Vaccination rates across Gaza have dropped from 96 to 80 percent, doctors said.

Cases of scabies, gastroenteritis, and Hepatitis A, among other infectious conditions, have risen dramatically, and without a sustained source of clean water, personal hygiene becomes greatly compromised.

Dr Munir al-Barsh, head of the water and environment sector in Gaza, said water well production has dropped to one-third of pre-7 October 2023 levels, but even that amount can be contaminated by waste and pollution that has seeped into the earth.

Desalination projects are largely out of service for drinking water, and fuel shortages are contributing to the inability to run machinery for these basic services.

Even chlorine, a crucial disinfectant, is prohibited from entering Gaza, Barsh said.

Israel maintains that between 600 and 800 trucks have clearance to enter Gaza with goods each day.

But the non-profit Refugees International has said that most of these trucks are carrying commercial goods brought in by private traders, not humanitarian supplies.

Invisible traumas

At least 73,066 Palestinians have been killed so far in the genocide, according to the Gaza health ministry - more than a thousand of them since the US declaration of the 10 October ceasefire.

"The total number of people killed or injured so far is quarter of a million. That is 12.5 percent of the population," Dr Mustafa Barghouti, president of the Palestinian Medical Relief Society, said on the call.

"Apply that number to the United States of America, and you will be talking about no less than 36 million people killed or injured. You can imagine what the United States would have done if one million Americans were injured or killed."

Among the invisible traumas are mental health conditions that now far exceed the capabilities of doctors and social workers in the enclave, most of whom have lost at least one person in their family to Israeli fire, Abdullah el-Jamal, a psychiatric consultant in Gaza, said.

"One day, I sent a case. She was a female, 32 years old, in severe depression and PTSD signs and symptoms, to one of my psychologists," he recounted. "She told me, Dr Abdullah, do you forget that I lost my son? Why you send me this case, [a] person like me?"

"We don't know how to deal with this situation," he added. "We need a lot of support."

Some two million people in Gaza are now displaced, and close to half a million buildings are deemed uninhabitable.

But Barghouti insists there is hope.

"We are steadfast," he said.

"Although we lost maybe almost 22,000 children in Gaza killed by the genocide... [there are now] 82,000 newborn children... These are signs of the resistance of the Palestinian people."

articles.title: China’s bathhouse boom: How a centuries-old tradition became a trendy 24-hour hangout
articles.summary: China’s bathhouses have evolved into all-day leisure destinations.
articles.content:
Letter From Beijing

China’s bathhouse boom: How a centuries-old tradition became a trendy 24-hour hangout

BEIJING – At noon on a Wednesday, a handful of people are soaking in steaming hot pools, while others lie on spa beds getting a vigorous scrub from attendants.

Upstairs, the dining hall is packed with diners dressed in identical loungewear, piling their plates high with fruit from the buffet line.

Elsewhere, couples and friend groups lounge on oversized beanbags with their iPads, play mahjong or sink into reclining armchairs for an afternoon nap.

Some will not leave until the next morning.

This is no hotel but a bathhouse in Beijing.

I’m at Shui Guo, one of the many Chinese bathhouses that resemble sprawling leisure complexes where visitors come to socialise, eat, work and unwind, with bathing just one part of the experience.

Once utilitarian places where people went to wash, China’s bathhouses have evolved into all-day leisure destinations, tapping younger consumers’ appetite for affordable experiences, while breathing new life into a centuries-old communal tradition.

In the process, they have become million-dollar, 24-hour wellness facilities popular with the younger generation and tourists, with one Chinese operator opening Singapore’s first large China-style bathhouse earlier in 2026.

I spent almost an entire day in Beijing’s Shui Guo in June and found myself among university students celebrating the end of their exams, office workers working on PowerPoint decks between trips to the sauna rooms and three-generation families making a day of it together.

On Chinese social media platform Xiaohongshu, bathhouses have become trendy destinations where young people pose for photos against stylish interiors or spend the day relaxing with family and friends.

Outside of China, videos on TikTok often describe them as “24-hour spas” or places to kill time between flights, recover after a day of sightseeing or simply experience a unique Chinese form of leisure.

Many of these establishments feature dining halls offering unlimited fruit and drink alongside steam and sauna rooms, cinemas, karaoke and game lounges, children’s play areas and rows of reclining chairs and semi-private sleeping pods – a package many young Chinese see as better value than a hotel stay or a short getaway.

At Shui Guo, a 16-hour weekday pass costs around 300 yuan (S$57). Weekend admission costs about the same, but the stay is limited to eight hours. For an extra 69 yuan, guests can stay the night and wake up to a complimentary breakfast buffet.

A cuozao, or body scrub that is long associated with Chinese bathhouses, costs an additional 200 yuan for a 40-minute session.

The all-in package drew June Wang, a 21-year-old Beijing Normal University student, and her two friends to Shui Guo Spa the day after they finished their examinations.

“We came here early because we wanted to maximise the 16-hour entry. It’s a little expensive for us on a student budget, but there are so many things we can do,” said Wang, a second-year undergraduate student.

“It’s nice to sit here with my friends to chat and do mindless things. It’s very relaxing.”

By late afternoon, the venue gradually filled with students on their summer break and office workers.

Outside, three other bathhouse complexes sit along the 300m-long street, each targeting a different clientele.

Shui Guo and neighbouring V Hot Spring cater to a younger crowd with stylish interiors designed as much for social media as for soaking. No. 9 Hot Spring Centre attracts families with an all-you-can-eat seafood buffet included in the ticket price of around 400 yuan.

Qushui Lanting caters to the more affluent and business crowd, with admission costing around 1,200 yuan. Caviar, lobster and foie gras are part of the buffet line.

A wealth management director who advises high-net-worth clients said Qushui Lanting is a popular spot for wealthy businessmen to discuss business in a relatively relaxed, semi-private setting.

“Everyone strips and changes into the same loungewear, so there’s a sense that we have nothing to hide from each other,” he quipped.

The trend is playing out across China, with modern bathhouse complexes opening in many major Chinese cities such as Beijing, Shanghai and Guangzhou. The Shui Guo chain, for instance, opened its first outlet in Beijing and now operates nine branches in six cities, with its newest in the southern tech hub of Shenzhen.

According to the 2025 China Leisure Bathhouse Industry Development White Paper, the country’s leisure bathhouse industry reached a market size of 120 billion yuan in 2025, up 10.5 per cent from a year earlier.

Data from Meituan, an online platform where bathhouse admission passes can be purchased, show that transaction volumes have increased every year over the last four years, peaking in 2025, which it described as a “boom year” for China’s bathhouse industry.

Year-on-year growth exceeded 20 per cent, making it one of the few service sectors to have sustained continuous growth in recent years.

These days, bathhouses are giving shopping malls, cinemas, restaurants, hotels and even weekend getaways a run for their money.

In Singapore, House+ Bubble, a $45 million Chinese-style spa complex opened earlier in 2026 in Singapore’s Jurong East, and features a cinema, meditation room and e-sports lounge, in addition to hot pools and saunas. Prices start at $59 for a six-hour entry.

From bathhouse to leisure complex

The bathhouse’s evolution has been centuries in the making.

Ancient texts suggest that bathing was already closely associated with hygiene, etiquette and ritual during the Qin Dynasty, with elaborate baths and hot spring retreats enjoyed by members of the imperial court in the later dynasties. Public bathhouses became more popular during the Song Dynasty, offering not only bathing but body scrubs, refreshments and places to rest.

Chinese historical dramas frequently depict nobles soaking in ornate wooden bathtubs, notably in recent hit Pursuit of Jade.

Kan Li, an assistant professor at Xi’an Jiaotong-Liverpool University’s department of China Studies, said the leisure and social functions of public bathhouses have remained remarkably enduring.

During the Republican era (1912-1949), growing awareness of public hygiene and urban living made bathhouses part of everyday life for many city residents, according to Li. Even then, many establishments offered body-scrubbing services, refreshments, and rest areas.

“Although the establishments of that era would have looked quite different from the sprawling leisure complexes we see today, bathing has always had a natural affinity with socialising and relaxation,” said Li, a historian of modern China.

It is thought that the current form of China’s bathhouse culture most likely originated in north-eastern China, which includes Liaoning, Jilin and Heilongjiang provinces, she added.

The region was China’s industrial base in the early decades of the People’s Republic, when large state-owned enterprises provided workers with spacious communal bathhouses as part of their welfare benefits.

When many of those facilities disappeared during state-sector restructuring in the 1990s, the habit of communal bathing endured, creating demand for commercial bathhouses, said Li.

Li, who grew up in northern China, recalled visiting these bathhouses as a child, where everyone changed into the same bathrobes before moving between hot pools and rest areas.

“This business model reflects not only the legacy of socialist collective life but also the influence of Japanese and Korean bathhouse culture,” she said.

Today, bathhouses continue to reflect regional traditions, although many operators increasingly blend them into a single experience.

One of them is cuozao, or body-scrubbing, traditionally most closely associated with northern arid China, while hot spring bathing is preferred in the warmer, more humid south.

Not for the faint-hearted, cuozao involves first a skin-softening soak in a heated pool before an attendant goes to work scrubbing away dead skin with a textured cloth from neck to toe.

It was far more vigorous than any spa treatment I’d experienced before, although oddly satisfying once it was over.

My attendant, who is in her 40s and identified herself only as No. 517, moved to Beijing from Gansu province in north-central China four years ago.

“Cuozao is not big in Gansu, so my family and friends are confused about why I decided to pick up this job,” she said. “But I like it because it’s a skill that can get me hired in almost every bathhouse in China.”

Bathhouse tourism

The body scrub, once a largely regional tradition, is now a popular draw for bathhouse customers, including foreigners.

Liu Simin, researcher at Beijing International Studies University’s China Culture and Tourism Research Centre, said the north-eastern bathhouse culture has become a tourism product in its own right, as domestic travellers seek out distinctive and authentic local experiences.

In north-east China, a boom in winter tourism has given rise to the nickname “little potatoes” for tourists from southern China, who travel north for the snow, local cuisine, and increasingly, the region’s famous bathhouses.

Liu sees the trend as part of China’s broader shift towards experience-led consumption. Many aspects of culture originated from basic needs such as eating, he noted.

“Once those needs are met, people transform them into culture and elevate them into a more sophisticated form of enjoyment,” he said.

It is also not surprising that young people have become an important customer group, as the one-stop-shop nature of bathhouses, coupled with the relatively low costs, provides good value for money, he said.

Barring high set-up capital and labour costs, Liu said the bathhouse model can be easily replicated as services are largely the same everywhere, with differences mainly in the design of the environment.

“It’s not high technology. It’s more like McDonald’s, except people are soaking in baths and getting massages. It can absolutely be standardised and expanded as a chain,” he said.

When I arrived at Shui Guo at 10am, I expected to spend a few hours working, trying a body scrub and leaving before dinner.

Instead, I left close to 10pm, freshly showered, relaxed and ready to do little more than collapse onto my sofa and put on Netflix.

As I was leaving, I saw a group of visitors arriving, some wheeling suitcases.

For me, the day was over. For them, it was only just beginning.

  • Additional reporting by Ingrid Yu
